paragraphs.body: I was 10 when I first experienced a disastrous hurricane. I heard the news on my grandma’s TV about how this hurricane kept increasing until it reached category 5. No one knew how to prepare for this. The gas stations had kilometric lines, grocery stores were filled at capacity, and the weather days before the hurricane made everything seem like it would be the end of the world. My family, my pets, and I stayed in the basement throughout the passage of Hurricane Maria. I remember I was sleeping, and suddenly, I heard my parents rushing to get the mop and towels because the floor started flooding. When it was safe to go outside, I remember how my 10-year-old self needed to cry. There were fallen trees, the fences were broken, and the air felt heavy; I couldn’t believe my home was destroyed. But what was important was that my family was safe, and we were together. We put batteries in our radio and heard the news for the first time. Thousands of Puerto Ricans lost their houses, pets, family members, and basically, all they had. Puerto Rico didn’t have any communication with the outside world for a few days. FEMA, the Federal Emergency Management Agency, came 90 days after the disaster. To top that off, President Trump brought the supplies we needed when he came to the island, but he just started throwing paper towels to the Puerto Ricans in need. To this day, there are Puerto Ricans without houses and struggling to restart their lives after Hurricane María.

annotations.comment: great descriptions. I'm still quite taken with them :)

paragraphs.body: Puerto Rico is an island in the Caribbean. It is 100 miles long and 35 miles wide. It has rich, green, humid rainforests, which are home to the coquis and other animals. Puerto Rico’s beaches are the most beautiful in the world. The water is crystalline, and the sun makes it paradise. Puerto Ricans are welcoming people who are always up for a good time. The food there is the best food you will ever try, from its traditional “arroz con gandules,” which is rice with pigeon peas, at parties to traditional desserts like “tembleque.” Sadly, after Maria, most of that was lost. Our lives changed to our entirety. We didn’t have electricity for 5 months, and we didn’t have water for 3 months. It made Puerto Ricans, my family, and I so much more resilient than ever. Even though we did struggle, my family and I tried our best to maintain our traditions. It took time for everything to return to normal, and we had parties for Christmas with many of our family members, with amazing and delicious food. We just enjoyed the time without worrying that we still didn’t have electricity or potable water. Seeing my family looking for the bright side in those very tough times made me realize how material things don’t matter as much when you are healthy and with your loved ones. It made me realize that my language isn’t only the Spanish we speak at home; it is the language of resilience in moments of difficulty. That language will help me in any tough times I encounter on whichever journey I decide to embark on.

My family plays a really important role in my life. As mentioned, my family ensured my siblings and I were happy during those tough moments. My parents, my siblings, and I lived in a house on top of my grandparents' house. Our house had two rooms (one of which my three siblings shared), a bathroom, a kitchen, and a living room. We grew up in a very tight-knit family. We did everything together. On one unexpected trip, my mother told my sister and me that she and my father were getting divorced. As soon as my mother told us the news, I started laughing. I couldn’t believe it. My family was falling apart. I would never have imagined that my parents would file for divorce. They seemed okay, but now that I look back on the situation, I realize how they managed it, always thinking about their children. I am grateful that my parents knew how to manage this tough situation. It was a rough time for me. I lost many friends along the way because I also changed. I thought my life would consist of whatever was happening during that period of my life. Later, that difficult period of my life made me so much stronger. It helped me visualize things from other perspectives that I wouldn’t have been able to see if it wasn’t for what happened with my family. This sad event, which changed my family forever, triggered this saying my family used to blurt out as soon as I had any inconvenience. "Después de la tormenta, viene la calma" / "After the storm, comes the calm”. This saying has helped me greatly in every difficult situation I have encountered, and I felt like a little boat encountering a tsunami.

paragraphs.body: Being in Pittsburgh, so far away from my home, has made me appreciate Puerto Rico and my family. It has also made me realize how Puerto Rico and my family have shaped me into the hardworking young woman I have become. I proudly say I am from Puerto Rico, “la Isla del Encanto” (the Island of Enchantment) everywhere I go. There is nothing greater than my pride in being a Puerto Rican woman. I dream of one day becoming a lawyer and helping my country obtain the justice it deserves. Although it may be confusing that I am studying in the United States, there is not a second in my day that passes by that I don’t remind myself that I am working as hard as I can, representing every Puerto Rican working harder back at home, including my family.

My wild tongue isn’t only Spanish. It is the category 5 hurricane that I survived. It is the maturity my parents have taught me in any situation, the resilience, and the hard work. I speak my wild tongue in every corner of the world, always having present where I came from, that the Puerto Rican race is not a race that gives up, and that my family is always backing me up in everything that I do. Constantly thinking about where I came from and why I am where I am motivated me every day to become the best version of myself and walk with my head up because I am a hard-working Puerto Rican, making my island proud of another amazing human being it produced and introduced to the rest of the world.

paragraphs.body: That hurricane changed my life. Back then, I thought everything would go down from there, but it was the opposite. It made us stronger; it made me stronger too. It was hard going back to normal, but I learned that we didn’t go back to that; we just made a new future and had so many experiences to learn from.

Puerto Rico, the Island of Enchantment To which I have a strong attachment Because it is where I grew up It taught me that no matter what I encounter I should maintain my chin up I miss Puerto Rico every day and it is hard being away But I look back to the island that raised me And, day to day, it inspires me to obtain my degree As the strong Puerto Rican chasing her dream

annotations.text: As the strong Puerto Rican chasing her dream
